common_name: Mosaica Copper Plant (Multi-Color Tropical Foliage Shrub for Gulf South Gardens)

planting.note: Plant container plants after the last frost in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Mosaica develops the most vivid multi-color leaf patterning in full sun — partial shade produces larger leaves but significantly reduces the intensity and complexity of the copper, red, orange, and yellow mosaic color pattern.

directions: Plant in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Mosaica is a cultivar of Acalypha wilkesiana (copper leaf, copperleaf, Jacob's coat) — a tropical shrub from the Pacific Islands grown primarily as a foliage plant for the extraordinary multi-color patterning of its large, broad leaves. The 'Mosaica' cultivar produces leaves with a complex, patchwork mosaic of copper, red-orange, bronze, gold-yellow, and reddish-purple tones — different colors appearing as irregular patches, blotches, and streaks across each leaf surface, creating a richly complex pattern that is genuinely different from plant to plant. The result in full sun is one of the most vivid and complex foliage colors available in a commonly grown landscape plant: a tropical tapestry of warm colors that creates intense visual interest in mixed subtropical plantings. Acalypha wilkesiana grows rapidly in Gulf South summer heat, reaching 4–6 feet in a single growing season when planted in warm soil with adequate nutrition. The inconspicuous catkin-type flowers are secondary to the foliage display. In zone 10–12, copper leaf is an evergreen perennial shrub. In zone 9, it grows as a warm-season annual — planted after the last frost and growing rapidly through summer and fall until frost kills it. The rapid growth and striking color make it one of the most effective single-season tropical foliage plants for Gulf South summer borders and container combinations.

common_name: Guest of Honor Camellia (Large Salmon-Pink Semi-Double Exhibition Japonica)

planting.note: Plant container plants in spring in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, strongly acidic (pH 4.5–6.0) soil in partial shade; Guest of Honor is a mid-season to late-season bloomer (March–April in zone 9), extending the camellia season later into spring when earlier-blooming varieties have finished.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, strongly acidic (pH 4.5–6.0) soil in partial shade; Guest of Honor is a well-established Camellia japonica cultivar producing large, semi-double flowers in warm salmon-pink — a color between clear pink and peach-salmon that is warmer and more orange-tinted than standard camellias, providing a distinctive color option in the predominantly pink-red-white camellia palette. The large, semi-double flowers (typically 4–5 inches) show a loose arrangement of wide, slightly wavy outer petals surrounding a center of mixed stamens and petaloids in the classic semi-double camellia form — less formal than fully double varieties but with more visual complexity than single-flowered types. The salmon-pink color stands out clearly against the characteristic glossy, deep-green evergreen foliage of camellia and is sufficiently distinctive that Guest of Honor is consistently recognized in the landscape. Guest of Honor grows as a medium-to-large, upright, densely branched evergreen shrub with vigorous growth in appropriate conditions. In Gulf South zone 9, it is one of the most reliable long-lived landscape camellias — a plant that improves with age and can become a large, impressive landscape feature over decades of growth. Strongly acidic soil (pH 4.5–6.0) and consistent moisture are the primary cultural requirements. The unusual salmon-pink color makes Guest of Honor an outstanding specimen where more typical pink or red camellias would seem repetitive.

common_name: Veitchii Gardenia (Classic Florist Gardenia with Everblooming Double White Flowers)

planting.note: Plant container plants in spring in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, strongly acidic (pH 4.5–5.5) soil in partial shade; Veitchii was historically the standard florist gardenia for cut flowers and corsages and produces more compact, more regularly shaped flowers than the larger, showier 'Fortuniana' — both fragrant but with different use profiles.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, strongly acidic (pH 4.5–5.5) soil in partial shade; Veitchii is the classic florist and corsage gardenia cultivar — the variety that shaped the public image of gardenia for most of the 20th century as the preferred white flower for corsages, wedding bouquets, and cut flower arrangements, valued for its perfectly formed, fully double, pure-white flowers and the intense, sweet, iconic gardenia fragrance that makes it one of the most recognizable flower scents in the world. The flowers of Veitchii are somewhat smaller than those of the larger-flowered 'Fortuniana' and 'Mystery' cultivars but are more precisely and symmetrically formed — tight spirals of white petals forming a perfect double flower that is simultaneously elegant and lush. The key practical advantage of Veitchii over earlier cultivars is repeat or continuous bloom (sometimes described as 'everblooming') — unlike once-blooming gardenias that flower only in spring, Veitchii produces flowers from late spring through summer and into fall, a trait that made it invaluable commercially. The intense gardenia fragrance is full and true across all blooming periods. The plant grows as a compact to medium evergreen shrub (3–5 feet) with the characteristically glossy, deep-green foliage of gardenia. In zone 9, Veitchii is somewhat cold-sensitive — mulching the roots and planting against a south-facing wall provides winter protection in zone 8–9a. Strongly acidic soil and iron availability are the most common cultural challenges.

common_name: Camellia Balsam (Double-Flowered Old-Fashioned Garden Balsam for Partial Shade)

planting.note: Direct sow seeds shallowly on the soil surface (they need light to germinate) after the last frost in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade; Camellia Balsam self-sows reliably in Gulf South gardens and can be treated as a self-renewing annual — allow some plants to set and drop seeds at the end of the season for a volunteer crop the following year.

directions: Direct sow or transplant after the last frost in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade; Camellia Balsam is the double-flowered form of Impatiens balsamina — the old-fashioned garden balsam that was a staple of cottage and kitchen gardens for centuries before modern Busy Lizzie impatiens (I. walleriana) largely replaced it in the nursery trade. Unlike the shade-only New Guinea and standard impatiens of modern horticulture, garden balsam (I. balsamina) is notably more heat and sun tolerant, performing in partial shade and surviving briefly in full sun if well-watered — a significant practical advantage for Gulf South gardeners. The double flowers of the 'Camellia' series are fully double, ruffled, and resembling small camellias or roses in their layered petal arrangement, clustered against the stems and leaves of the upright plant rather than held above the foliage as in other impatiens. The flowers are produced along the main stem and branches throughout the season in colors ranging from white, pink, rose, red, salmon, lilac, and mixed bicolors. The explosive, snap-open seed pods that fling seeds several feet when touched — giving rise to the genus name Impatiens ('impatient') — are a source of curiosity and delight for children in the garden. Garden balsam is more heat-tolerant and self-sows more reliably in zone 9 than modern impatiens varieties, making it a more sustainable choice for Gulf South partial-shade annual gardens.

common_name: White Mexican Petunia (Compact White-Flowered Ruellia for Gulf South Borders)

planting.note: Plant container plants in spring in well-drained, lean to fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; note that Ruellia simplex (including white-flowered cultivars) is listed as invasive in Florida due to prolific seeding — plant responsibly in Florida and consider sterile or non-seeding dwarf cultivars in sensitive areas.

directions: Plant in well-drained, lean to fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; White Mexican Petunia is the white-flowered form of Ruellia simplex — a perennial Gulf South garden standard valued for extreme drought tolerance, long bloom season, and adaptability from full sun to deep shade, producing funnel-shaped, petunia-like flowers in pure white from spring through hard frost. The white flower color is cleaner and more visually versatile than the dominant purple of the straight species — more useful in moonlight gardens, white-themed borders, and combinations with deep blue or rich red companions where purple flowers would reduce contrast. Like all Ruellia simplex cultivars, the white-flowered form performs through brutal Gulf South summer heat without wilting, stopping, or requiring deadheading — the flowers drop cleanly in the morning and new blooms open daily throughout the growing season. The extreme heat, drought, and shade tolerance make Ruellia simplex uniquely versatile among perennial flowers: few plants perform acceptably in the full range from full sun to deep shade AND from drought to seasonal flooding. In zone 9, Mexican petunia grows as a reliable perennial, returning from the roots each spring and expanding by spreading rhizomes and self-seeding. The seeding behavior should be noted: the species is considered invasive in Florida wetlands and naturalized areas; plant responsibly and deadhead before seed set in ecologically sensitive areas. Outside of Florida, white Ruellia is widely used and appreciated as a versatile, near-indestructible perennial for difficult Gulf South garden positions.
